posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
There are close to 80 owners in Echezeaux - and some 20 in Grand-Echezeaux, with many negociant bottlings in addition, so hard to have an overview …

So I´m asking to list the best producers for each vineyard FOR THE MONEY … not the absolutely best - which in many cases will be:
DRC (E + GE)
Henry Jayer (E)
E.Rouget (E)
L-M Liger-Belair (E)

followed by:
Grivot
Meo-C
Dujac
Mugneret-Gibourg
Arnoux
Eugenie (former Engel)
…
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

I don’t think the money is a problem here. If you have the money, DRC/Liger Belair are the best to me and you will pay for them.
Tier 2 would be the best for the money I guess.

Thoughts about Domaine Anne Gros and Rene Engel?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
I think for Echezeaux and Grands Echezeaux that DRC stand head and shoulders above the pack because of the linage they have. They have been making brilliant wines from these two vineyards for many decades.

For Echezaux I reckon Jayer-Gilles may just be in that second tier. I have had a few aged ones that have been superb and recently a 2000 and 1999 have been quite magnificent.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I really love Bocquenet Echezeaux. It’s much more rustic than some (most?), but it ages into something wonderful.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
One more name to throw out there in the second tier for Echezeaux is Gerard Mugneret. Pretty decent value too. These are elegant beautiful wines. His Echezeaux comes from the same area as the sisters I think. (They are cousins I believe and he does some vineyard work for them.). Very nice stuff.

I too also like Jayer-Gilles quite a bit. I had a 1993 a few years ago that was out of sight.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
“For the money”, the Mugneret-Gibourg is tops, in my view. (Though the Rouget example is tops…when not considering “the money” or maybe considering it) for Echezaux.

I long considered the Engel Grands-Echezaux among the very top wines of Burgundy. Though I have no idea what Eugenie has done with these holdings, they certainly have the potential to continue this greatness. Never that impressed with Mongeard wines, but…no experience with them after 2000. Have liked the DRC Grands-Echezaux fine on those rare occasions I’ve had DRC wines. Don’t think of it as good value though…or really anything with the DRC label on it. Added worth are things extrinsic to the actual wines, IMO. No opinion on that value to others…never was of any worth to me and my goals, though, especially at the Echezaux/G-E level. Am frank to admit to very little experience with DRC as a result, so I can’t argue much about the subject of DRC (and some other similar estates/businesses).

posts.name: Bryan Thomas
posts.cooked:
Jayer Gilles with 15+ years on it is very much worth a look - may offer one of the best values for top quality wine. Gets a bad wrap with the toasty oak which seems to integrate beautifully with time.

Mugneret Gibourg never seems to disappoint - only thing that is disappointing is the price increase this estate has seen over the last several years.

Cecile Tremblay needs to be on anyones short list of top Echezeaux producers - too bad it is nearly impossible to find!

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:

Agree on all these points. Love wines from both Mugneret Gibourg and Cecile Tremblay. Too bad they are so expensive.

In Germany, we get ok availability on Cecile Tremblay and the price for her Echézeaux - while not cheap - is at least not outrageous (2011 = 140 Euros / bt). How good is it? I never had it. I like Tremblays Vosne Romanée VV, but I don’t buy it at the price asked for (> 50 Euros / bt).

For “value” I like the Echezeaux from Regis Forey. I like the style and it ages really well. It’s crept up in price a bit and is now around 85 Euros / bt., but it used to be at just over 60 Euros / bt. - a great price for that wine. It’s certainly not among the best, but it’s really good in my view.

Confuron-Cotetidot make a delightful example. Much like their Clos Vougeot the parcel is not located in the most revered terroir (Les Treux) but that doesn’t carry through to the final product. Tremendous success year in and year out.

posts.name: paul clark
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

“For the money”, the Mugneret-Gibourg is tops, in my view. (Though the Rouget example is tops…when not considering “the money” or maybe considering it) for Echezaux.

Hedonism Wines had the 2009 E Rouget on tap this afternoon. I couldn’t pass a taste given the relevance of this thread.

Holy cow. Any oak influence on this wine is consumed by the shear size and complexity of this wine. It’s so powerful but so fresh. It’s so massive but it floats right across the palate weightlessly.

I’ve clearly underestimated the top of the class in this vineyard. I never imagined the gap between the the M-G’s and C-C’s would be so wide. Shockingly good wine.

posts.name: Thierry Jutel
posts.cooked:
dcornutt:
Kirk.Grant:

Thoughts about Domaine Anne Gros and Rene Engel?

Engels are no longer made. Very nice Grands Echezeaux from Philippe. The 99 is a monumental wine. 93 is also incredible.
Anne Gros I don’t have any experience.

I tasted the Anne Gros les Echezeaux Les Loachausses recently and here are my notes.

Domaine Anne Gros Echezeaux Les Loachausses 2007
Evolved colour. Lifted pure fruit. Red cherry, great florality and some stalkyness. Crisp bright fruit with good structure; quite velevety. Not a blockbuster but good expressiveness. Good length and structure with a nice focusing acid bite at the end in the form of sour cherry.

Domaine Anne Gros Echezeaux Les Loachausses 2009
More flamboyant and more lifted, perfumed with oak well integrated. A lot more concentration and material with grippy tannins. Quite shut down and tightly wound but potentially very interesting. Whole bunch character coming through.

I liked the 2009 a lot and bought some.

posts.name: Thierry Jutel
posts.cooked:
dcornutt:
Kirk.Grant:

Thoughts about Domaine Anne Gros and Rene Engel?

Engels are no longer made. Very nice Grands Echezeaux from Philippe. The 99 is a monumental wine. 93 is also incredible.
Anne Gros I don’t have any experience.

I tasted the Anne Gros les Echezeaux Les Loachausses recently and here are my notes.

Domaine Anne Gros Echezeaux Les Loachausses 2007
Evolved colour. Lifted pure fruit. Red cherry, great florality and some stalkyness. Crisp bright fruit with good structure; quite velevety. Not a blockbuster but good expressiveness. Good length and structure with a nice focusing acid bite at the end in the form of sour cherry.

Domaine Anne Gros Echezeaux Les Loachausses 2009
More flamboyant and more lifted, perfumed with oak well integrated. A lot more concentration and material with grippy tannins. Quite shut down and tightly wound but potentially very interesting. Whole bunch character coming through.

I liked the 2009 a lot and bought some. And the price was very appealing.

posts.name: Alan Factor

I would have to put Rouget and Mugneret-Gibourg as my favorite Echezeaux producers after DRC. At the next price level I would Forey and Bocquenet at the top. It’s hard to evaluate Liger-Belair and Anne Gros, both only producing for under a decade.

Grands-Ecnezeaux favorites are probably Lamarche (since Nicole took charge) and Drouhin. The old Maison Leroy bottles from the 50s and 60s are magical.

posts.cooked:

One of the few E´s that can compete with Rouget is Liger-Belair (Comte) …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Not sure if the gap is “huge”, but surely agree that there is a gap between the Mugneret Echezaux and the Jayer/Rouget version, which is one of the greatest wines in all of Burgundy, to me.

In fairness to the Mugnerets…since I first visited in 1988 (Dr. Georges was then very sick), their Echezaux was always the 3rd best wine there, behind the Clos Vougeot and Ruchottes-Chambertin (not sure which of those is usually better). It has always been good, and has improved much since 1988 , but is still third best even there.

By contrast, IMO, the Echezaux chez Jayer/Rouget (which is a wholly different style of winemaking from Mugneret…which stresses finesse)…is the top wine there, though the Cros Parantoux is a trophier wine, for sure. It is always great there…as is the VR Beaumonts…The Cros P is more expensive and more coveted, but…their Echezaux is the best example of Echezaux I know (and the Daniel Rion estate makes a nice one, too).

So…with that in mind and with the more opulent winemaking signature of Jayer/Rouget…clearly a “step up” for the latter.

How do you compare , “step up”-wise the Rouget Echezaux to the Mugnerets’ Ruchottes or CV, Gerhard?

posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

It has always been good, and has improved much since 1988 , but is still third best even there.

From the EP pricing, CV and Ruchottes are priced the same and always higher than the Ech.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Not sure if the gap is “huge”, but surely agree that there is a gap between the Mugneret Echezaux and the Jayer/Rouget version, which is one of the greatest wines in all of Burgundy, to me.

In fairness to the Mugnerets…since I first visited in 1988 (Dr. Georges was then very sick), their Echezaux was always the 3rd best wine there, behind the Clos Vougeot and Ruchottes-Chambertin.
…
By contrast, IMO, the Echezaux chez Jayer/Rouget (which is a wholly different style of winemaking from Mugneret…which stresses finesse)…is the top wine there, though the Cros Parantoux is a trophier wine, for sure.
…

How do you compare , “step up”-wise the Rouget Echezaux to the Mugnerets’ Ruchottes or CV, Gerhard?

Stuart,

I haven´t visited M-G after 2002 - and nothing tasted after 2007, with the exception of one Gevrey 1er Cru ´11 …

I agree that (at Mugneret-Giburg) the CV and Ruchottes-Ch was/is slightly superior to the Echezeaux … the E. often had a certain “stemminess” (even if it´s destemmed) and more prominent acidity - both not necessarily negative - but I felt CV + R-Ch were simply more intense … one reason why I always bought these - and rarely the E (and also it was always difficult with two extra invoices for the “Georges Mugneret”-domaine and the “Mugneret-Gibourg”-domaine … which now has been solved). I used to prefer the CV, but not always.
(BTW: the Ruchottes has recently been replanted and delassified to Gevrey 1er Cru … is there a Ruchottes again ?)

I disagree that the M-G-style has more finesse than Rouget, rather the opposite. What I admire at Rouget is his perfect combination of finesse and intensity, without heaviness … and always the power and structure in the background, very elegant fruit in the foreground, beautiful perfumed. The wood integrates beautifully over time. The wines simply have great class and style in a very natural way.
For me the M-G wines feel “heavier”, less natural and sometimes with a slightly alcoholic touch … but nevertheless I really like the textbook beetroot/tomatoe/red-berries-fruit - all comparisons of course on a very high level …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: There was always Ruchottes. The young vine portion was bottles separately as a 1er for several years, but that now is mature enough to be included in the Ruchottes, as of 2012.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

ng from Mugneret…which stresses finesse)…is the top wine there, though the Cros Parantoux i

I disagree that the M-G-style has more finesse than Rouget, rather the opposite. What I admire at Rouget is his perfect combination of finesse and intensity, without heaviness … and always the power and structure in the background, very elegant fruit in the foreground, beautiful perfumed. The wood integrates beautifully over time. The wines simply have great class and style in a very natural way.
For me the M-G wines feel “heavier”, less natural and sometimes with a slightly alcoholic touch … but nevertheless I really like the textbook beetroot/tomatoe/red-berries-fruit - all comparisons of course on a very high level …

Though I prefer the Rouget version, Gerhard, i think it is more about power, extract and oak than the Mugneret version (which did have a stemmy aspect often when Dr. Georges made it…and even later). To me “power, extract and obvious new oak” is not finesse…sort of the opposite. A heavy signature style, albeit a tasty one, and impressive.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Though I prefer the Rouget version, Gerhard, i think it is more about power, extract and oak than the Mugneret version (which did have a stemmy aspect often when Dr. Georges made it…and even later). To me “power, extract and obvious new oak” is not finesse…sort of the opposite. A heavy signature style, albeit a tasty one, and impressive.

Stuart,

we should agree to disagree on this matter…

BTW: finesse and power are no opposits … can well be combined in a specific wine …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
With Grivot 2012 echezeaux offered at 200 £/b ib in London early 2014, it has joined the club of grossly overpriced (echezeaux is 2nd class) Grand cru Echezeaux. No dispute the wine is good…
Any suggestion for a less than 100 €/bottle echezeaux of good quality:
Confuron Gindre, Clerget, Forey, Vigot, Bichot, Confuron Cot…?

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:

we should agree to disagree on this matter…

BTW: finesse and power are no opposits … can well be combined in a specific wine …

We’re talking about semantics here.

Finesse, to me, is an overall effect, grounded on “delicacy”. Finesse Definition & Meaning - Merriam-Webster

100% new oak; long pre-fermentation soak, etc. can make great wines; however, they are unlikely to be wines based on delicacy or finesse…as these two practices alone thwart that. As much as I might like the result…and as fine as the wine can turn out…they are not delicate wines when made this way, IMO. And, I’ve never had one from Jayer/Rouget…that I thought was a wine primarily of finesse…not even Rouget’s savigny-les- Beaune.

Many of my favorite producers’ wines are not wines of “finesse” as i understand the term. So, I don’t look at such wines negatively.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
A Songeur:

Any suggestion for a less than 100 €/bottle echezeaux of good quality:

The Domaine Daniel Rion, which I used to follow and admire until Patrice Rion formed his own domaine in 2001, makes a really nice Echezaux and has for some time now…a check on Wine-Searcher reveals that the 2010 costs $110 at LeDu wines in NYC.

Had the 15’ Desaunay-Bissey Vosne 1er Beaux Monts on release a few years back and couldn’t believe how good it was. Grabbed a case, then thought to try their Eche and Grands Eche since the Beaux Monts was so good. Ended up a disappointment, the Eche was ok but lacking in tension and complexity, wasn’t really a wine I wanted to drink. The Grands Eche was even worse, grapey and clumsy, again no tension or interest. Wouldn’t rule out the possibility of them improving with age, but the wines seemed fairly opened, and I’d never bet on them.

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan

Answering your question I would add that both 2014 and 2015 were splendid vintages at JM MILLOT. Contrary to my colleague I think the 2014s will be ready rather early. Sooner than the 2015s to be sure. Alix MILLOT could become another great name in BURGUNDY. We all know MADAME “Trop Chère” (however splendid her wines may be and they are brilliant. I was a client there in the nineties). Nathalie LAMARCHE makes brilliant wines too. ANNE GROS is underestimated now. Has a bit to do with her buying vineyards in MINERVOIS which are also for sale in BURGUNDY. Mathilde GRIVOT in the near future succeeding to the well loved ETIENNE???. I always wonderded why she didn’t marry Charles LAChaux so two great domaines could be united…). The last remark was a joke…
SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I recently bought a few bottles of the 15 Desaunay-Bissey VR Beaux Mont based on a steller review by William Kelley for this particular wine (and very high cellar-tracker reviews.) I greatly respect William Kelley’s palate, knowledge and opinions and he does contribute to this board frequently. I wonder if he could chime in on the inconsistency of his review and the poor showing of the Echezeaux and G E, if he also tasted them as well.

I am enjoying this thread…

Kind of you to say! I tasted some of the 2015s from bottle in the US, not at the domaine, so I don’t have too much context. I liked the 2015 Beaux Monts that you bought a lot, and it sounds as if Robert agreed with that assessment: I hope it lives up to the review when you try a bottle. The Echézeaux, when I tasted it, was also impressive; but with more puppy fat and mid-palate flesh than the Beaux Monts, so I can see why someone (i.e. Robert) might have liked it less. I didn’t taste the Grands Echezeaux. This estate has lots of old vine holdings in top appellations but also a reputation for being a little inconsistent. At some point I’d like to visit but there are so many domaines in Burgundy, as you know, and only so much time…

The Jayer-Gilles Eschezeaux has been reliably excellent over the years. The winery and vineyards were purchased in 2017 by Andre Hoffman, and the wines are now produced by a new team under the name Hoffman-Jayer. Mr. Jayer Gilles passed away a couple of years ago, sorry to say. If anyone has visited the domaine recently, or tasted a new release, please post some notes.

Echézeaux du Dessus Jayer-Gilles 1999 : do you think it is ready to drink ?

I thought it was showing beautifully in early March.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The Jayer-Gilles Eschezeaux has been reliably excellent over the years. The winery and vineyards were purchased in 2017 by Andre Hoffman, and the wines are now produced by a new team under the name Hoffman-Jayer. Mr. Jayer Gilles passed away a couple of years ago, sorry to say. If anyone has visited the domaine recently, or tasted a new release, please post some notes.

I’ve tasted 2017s and 2018s. The style hasn’t really changed: 100% destemmed, picked ripe, 100% new oak, very long élevage. The wines are quite saturated in hue, with plenty of rich extract, but do digest the oak—even the Hautes-Côtes bottlings, which I’d recommend if you like the style—with time in barrel and bottle.

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Friends,

Regarding the DESAUNEY-BISSEy domaine I must add that, following Wiliam KELLEY’s rather positive evaluation, CLIVE COATES also liked their wines; his evaluation came after I asked him a question if he’d ever heard of the domaine. I do not know what vintage he tasted but I stick to my original opinion having tasted 3 millésimes: mediocre. Even in a great vintage (2002). if anyone has a wide positive experience with their wines then I would like to hear of it. Maybe there has been a changing of the guard there lately. I must, however, refer to what PITIOT at CLOS de TART once told me :“All those domaines you never heard of…There must be a reason. Why is the name ROUSSEAU a household name? Because everybody knows it’s sublime.” If WILLIAM KELLEY does not plan a visit it is because he does not expect to find great quality there??? True or not??? Why do wine writers never visit domaine CAMUS in GEVREY? It has smashingly good vinyards??? Because they know it will be mediocre…

SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: A Songeur

Never opened one of my Mugneret Gibourg as they are too young. I love Grivot Echezeaux (even the 2004 was excellent). I found JM Millot Echezeaux 2010 fine, nicely balanced, delicious with some length… but very simple (little depth, no complexity), not a grand cru according to the few bottles I have had ( 5 down, 5 left). Others have been decent (Potel, Clerget, Rion,…) but not stellar. So, yes, producer is paramount.

… and a Dujac and a DRC 1988 were out of this world…

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Friends,

Just returned from a visit to a Corona-threatened BURGUNDY. I already mentioned in some thread that Alix Millot is one of my favorite producers. Very interesting is that she makes 2 different ECHEZEAUX. A regular cuvée and ECHEZEAUX du DESSUS. Although estimed tasters such as STEEN OHMAN seem to prefer the “DESSUS” I beg to differ. The regular cuvée is in her typical style : full of finesse and grace. Maybe not as concentraded, less of a “big” wine. Like it. Both can be found at the domaine provided you are a regular there…

SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng

Never opened one of my Mugneret Gibourg as they are too young. I love Grivot Echezeaux (even the 2004 was excellent). I found JM Millot Echezeaux 2010 fine, nicely balanced, delicious with some length… but very simple (little depth, no complexity), not a grand cru according to the few bottles I have had ( 5 down, 5 left). Others have been decent (Potel, Clerget, Rion,…) but not stellar. So, yes, producer is paramount.

… and a Dujac and a DRC 1988 were out of this world…

I think millot has changed in the last 5 years or so with winemaker change.

The resurrection of a very old thread. In the last few vintages, the Echézeaux of Jacques Prieur has been absolutely outstanding - in all company. The 19 is currently a bit tight - like all their Nuits wines - but anyone looking for the best should also look carefully at this bottling. Young, it’s always served after their Chambertin at the domaine, and one year, even after their Musigny!
